ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general jellyfish food product class; no specific entry for vinegar-marinated Qingdao jellyfish head preparation exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJM covers jellyfish as a food commodity. No specific code exists for the vinegar-marinated Qingdao preparation; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for jellyfish food product or vinegar-marinated jellyfish preparation.

preparation_method: seasoned with sesame oil and garlic

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Qingdao Vinegar Jellyfish is approximately 150 g, presented as a cold appetizer plate of sliced jellyfish head dressed in aged black vinegar, sesame oil, and garlic.

nutrient_source.title: Jellyfish, prepared (marinated/seasoned) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table; adapted with reference to published Chinese food composition data for marinated jellyfish
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for Qingdao Vinegar Jellyfish. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entry for jellyfish (raw/prepared, NDB 15244) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA data for marinated jellyfish and published Chinese food composition tables (Yang Yuexin et al., China Food Composition Table, 6th ed., 2018). Sodium is elevated due to soy sauce and vinegar marinade. Macros reflect the dressed, ready-to-eat product.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate for marinated jellyfish based on very low carbohydrate seafood/protein food category
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Qingdao Vinegar Jellyfish. Jellyfish is a very low-carbohydrate, high-protein seafood. GI is estimated at 15 based on analogy with other low-carbohydrate marinated seafood and protein-dominant foods in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (15 × 2.2 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 0.5, confirming negligible glycemic impact. gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and vinegar marinade (~520 mg sodium per 100 g; ~780 mg per 150 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Qingdao Vinegar Jellyfish due to its high sodium content (~780 mg per serving), which represents approximately 34% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit recommended by clinical guidelines; portion restriction or a low-sodium preparation is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (2.2 g per 100 g) and negligible glycemic load (GL ≈ 0.5 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Qingdao Vinegar Jellyfish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a cold appetizer, given its very low carbohydrate content and negligible glycemic load; however, sodium intake from the marinade should be monitored in patients with concurrent hypertension or diabetic nephropathy.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium content poses cardiovascular risk; low saturated fat and low caloric density are favorable
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While Qingdao Vinegar Jellyfish is low in saturated fat and calories, its high sodium content (~780 mg per serving) is a concern for cardiovascular disease management, and patients should limit consumption or request reduced-sodium preparations in line with AHA dietary sodium guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Qingdao Vinegar Jellyfish (青岛老醋蛰头, Qīngdǎo lǎo cù zhē tóu) is a classic cold appetizer originating from Qingdao, Shandong Province, China. The dish features the head portion of jellyfish (蛰头), which has a firm, crunchy, and slightly chewy texture, marinated in aged black vinegar (老醋), sesame oil, garlic, soy sauce, and a touch of sugar. The aged black vinegar, a hallmark of Shandong cuisine, imparts a deep, mellow sourness that distinguishes this preparation from simpler vinegar-dressed jellyfish dishes found elsewhere in China. The dish is served cold and is prized for its refreshing, tangy flavour and satisfying crunch. Nutritionally, jellyfish is very low in calories (~33 kcal per 100 g), low in fat, and provides a moderate amount of protein (~5.3 g per 100 g), making it a light and protein-containing appetizer. Carbohydrate content is minimal (~2.2 g per 100 g), resulting in a negligible glycemic load, which makes it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The primary clinical concern is its high sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g) derived from the soy sauce and vinegar marinade, which is relevant for patients with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or diabetic nephropathy. In Singapore, this dish is served in Chinese restaurants specialising in Shandong or northern Chinese cuisine and is commonly ordered as a starter or shared cold dish.
common_user_queries: How many calories in vinegar jellyfish?
Is vinegar jellyfish suitable for diabetics?
What is 青岛老醋蛰头?
What is the GI of jellyfish?
Can I eat jellyfish if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Qingdao Vinegar Jellyfish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA jellyfish data (NDB 15244) and cross-referenced with Chinese food composition tables (Yang Yuexin et al., 2018). GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is medium rather than low because the core ingredient (jellyfish) has well-characterised nutritional properties and the adaptation is well-supported.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central jellyfish entry and Chinese food composition data; no exact Singapore restaurant-specific analysis is available. 2. Sodium value (~520 mg/100 g) reflects the marinated, dressed product including soy sauce and vinegar; raw jellyfish sodium is lower. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero: jellyfish contains no dietary fibre by nature (naturally zero). 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per source data for this preparation. 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data for jellyfish. 6. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. 7. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. 8. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. 9. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. 10. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. 11. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. 12. GI is estimated at 15 based on analogy with low-carbohydrate marinated seafood; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 13. Halal status is unconfirmed — jellyfish is generally considered halal by most Islamic scholars but the marinade ingredients (soy sauce, vinegar) require halal certification verification. 14. The dish name uses 蛰头 (zhē tóu) in the input; the standard character is 蜇头 (zhē tóu); both forms are in common use and are treated as equivalent aliases.
